articles/ControllingSymbolDiscovery.html.title: Controlling Symbol Discovery | UCalc Documentation
articles/ControllingSymbolDiscovery.html.summary: Controlling Symbol Discovery UCalc automatically discovers symbols from types and exposes them to expressions. In most cases the default behavior works well, but you can customize it in several ways. Hiding Members If a member should not be visible inside expressions, add the UCalcHidden attribute. public class Character { public float Health; [UCalcHidden] public int InternalState; } The InternalState field will not appear in autocomplete and cannot be accessed in expressions. This works for: fields properties methods Making Members Read-Only If a field or property should be accessible but not writable, use the UCalcReadOnly attribute. public class Character { [UCalcReadOnly] public float MaxHealth; } Expressions can read the value: MaxHealth But assignments to the member will not be allowed. Project Settings Additional symbol discovery behavior can be configured in Project Settings → UCalc. Available options include: Include non-public instance members Include non-public static members These settings control whether private, protected and internal members are considered during symbol discovery. Custom Member Filters For more advanced control, you can register custom filters that determine which members are exposed. This is done by implementing the IUCalcMemberFilter interface. public class MyFilter : IUCalcMemberFilter { public bool AllowField(TypeContext ctx, FieldInfo field) { return !field.Name.StartsWith("_"); } public bool AllowProperty(TypeContext ctx, PropertyInfo prop) { return true; } public bool AllowMethod(TypeContext ctx, MethodInfo method) { return true; } } Then register the filter through UCalcMemberFilterRegistry. UCalcMemberFilterRegistry.AddGlobalMemberFilter(new MyFilter()); You can also register filters for a specific type: UCalcMemberFilterRegistry.AddTypeMemberFilter(typeof(MyType), new MyFilter()); Initialization Timing Filters should be registered early during initialization. This ensures they are applied before symbol discovery runs for a given type. Good places to register filters include: static initialization editor initialization methods runtime startup code If symbol discovery already happened for a type, your filter may not affect that cached context. When Should You Use Custom Filters? Custom filters are useful when you want to: hide members based on naming conventions restrict certain types expose only a limited API to designers implement project-specific safety rules

articles/ExpressionSyntax.html.title: Expression Syntax | UCalc Documentation
articles/ExpressionSyntax.html.summary: Expression Syntax UCalc expressions are compiled into .NET expression trees and support a subset of familiar C# syntax designed to be intuitive and efficient. This page lists all currently supported language features. Literals Expressions can contain literal values. Numeric literals 10 3.14 -5 Numeric literals are parsed using the configured culture. Boolean literals true false Null literal null Default literal The default keyword resolves to the most appropriate type depending on context. Example: value = default If value is float, the expression resolves default to: 0f Identifiers Identifiers refer to symbols available in the expression context. These may include: expression arguments owner members dependencies global symbols Example: health damageMultiplier enemy.Health Mathf.PI Autocomplete suggestions are provided in the editor while typing. Unary Operators Unary operators operate on a single value. Supported unary operators: Operator Description + Unary plus - Numeric negation ! Logical NOT ~ Bitwise NOT Example: -health !isAlive ~mask Binary Operators Binary operators operate on two values. Arithmetic Operator Description + Addition - Subtraction * Multiplication ** Exponentiation / Division % Modulo Example: health * damageMultiplier + bonus Comparison Operator Description == Equal != Not equal > Greater than < Less than >= Greater or equal <= Less or equal Example: health <= 0 Logical operators Operator Description && Logical AND || Logical OR Example: isAlive && stamina > 0 Bitwise operators Operator Description & Bitwise AND | Bitwise OR ^ Bitwise XOR << Bit shift left >> Bit shift right Example: mask & layer Function Calls Functions can be invoked using standard call syntax. functionName(arg1, arg2) Example: Mathf.Clamp(value, 0, 1) Functions support overloads, and the best overload is selected based on argument types. Autocomplete suggestions show available overloads in the editor. Constructors Objects can be created using the new keyword. new TypeName(arg1, arg2) Example: new Vector3(x, y, z) Constructors support overload resolution, similar to function calls. Indexing Index expressions allow accessing elements using indexers. collection[index] Example: inventory[0] grid[x] Indexers are resolved based on available indexer properties. Ternary Operator Conditional expressions use the ternary operator: condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse Example: health > 0 ? aliveScore : deadScore Optional else branch The else branch is optional. This allows ternary expressions to be used for conditional actions. Example: health <= 0 ? Die() Operator Precedence Operators follow standard precedence rules similar to C#: Unary operators Multiplication / division Addition / subtraction Comparisons Logical operators Ternary operator Parentheses can be used to explicitly control evaluation order. Example: (a + b) * c

articles/GettingStarted.html.title: Getting Started | UCalc Documentation
articles/GettingStarted.html.summary: Getting Started UCalc is designed to be simple to integrate and easy to use directly from the Unity Inspector. In most cases you only need to add one of the provided serializable expression types to your component and write the expression in the editor. Available Serialized Types UCalc provides several serialized expression types that can be used in your scripts. SerializedAction Represents a lambda that does not return a value. Available variants: SerializedAction SerializedAction<T1> SerializedAction<T1, T2> SerializedAction<T1, T2, T3> Example: [SerializeField] private SerializedAction<float> onDamage; SerializedFunc Represents a lambda that returns a value. Available variants: SerializedFunc<TResult> SerializedFunc<T1, TResult> SerializedFunc<T1, T2, TResult> SerializedFunc<T1, T2, T3, TResult> Example: [SerializeField] private SerializedFunc<float, float> damageFormula; UCalcEvent Represents an event with persistent lambda listeners. Available variants: UCalcEvent UCalcEvent<T1> UCalcEvent<T1, T2> UCalcEvent<T1, T2, T3> Example: [SerializeField] private UCalcEvent<float> onHealthChanged; Custom Argument Names Generic expressions use default argument names such as: arg0, arg1, arg2... You can override these names using the ArgNames attribute: [ArgNames("damage", "armor")] [SerializeField] private SerializedFunc<float, float, float> damageFormula; This makes the expression easier to read in the editor. Writing Expressions in the Inspector Once a serialized lambda field is added to your component, the Unity Inspector will display an input field where you can write the expression. UCalc provides autocomplete suggestions while typing and displays errors directly in the inspector if the expression cannot be compiled. Expression Owner Every serialized expression has an Owner (UnityEngine.Object). By default this is the component containing the expression. The expression has direct access to the owner's: public fields public properties public methods Example: health * damageMultiplier If health and damageMultiplier are public members on the owner, they can be referenced directly. Referencing Other Objects If your expression needs to reference other objects, you can add them as dependencies. Click the chain icon next to the expression label to expand the dependencies section. Then: Assign the object reference Provide a variable name You can now reference this object in the expression: enemy.Health * multiplier Global Symbols Expressions also have access to global symbols provided by static classes. For example: Mathf.Clamp(value, 0, 100) Classes like Mathf are exposed globally so their static members can be used directly. More information about extending the expression environment can be found in: Global Context Providers Error Reporting If an expression fails to compile, the editor will display an error message directly next to the field. This helps quickly identify syntax issues or missing symbols.

articles/GlobalSymbols.html.title: Global Symbols | UCalc Documentation
articles/GlobalSymbols.html.summary: Global Symbols UCalc allows you to expose additional global symbols that can be accessed from any expression. This is done by creating a static class and marking it with the UCalcGlobalContext attribute. All supported static members of that class will automatically become available inside expressions. Basic Example [UCalcGlobalContext] public static class DamageConstants { public const float CriticalMultiplier = 2f; public const float HeadshotMultiplier = 1.5f; } This exposes the symbols under the class name. Example usage in expressions: DamageConstants.CriticalMultiplier Custom Path You can specify a custom path in the attribute: [UCalcGlobalContext("Combat.DamageConstants")] public static class DamageConstants { public const float CriticalMultiplier = 2f; } This makes the symbols available under the specified namespace-like path: Combat.DamageConstants.CriticalMultiplier This is useful for organizing symbols into logical groups. No Prefix If the path is set to an empty string, symbols are exposed without a prefix. [UCalcGlobalContext("")] public static class DamageConstants { public const float CriticalMultiplier = 2f; } Now the symbols can be used directly: CriticalMultiplier Supported Members The following static members are exposed as symbols: fields properties methods These members can then be used inside expressions just like built-in symbols. Example: Mathf.Clamp(value, 0, DamageConstants.CriticalMultiplier) When Are Global Symbols Discovered? Global symbol providers are automatically discovered when the system initializes. Any static class marked with UCalcGlobalContext will be registered and made available to expressions.

articles/ProjectSettings.html.title: Project Settings | UCalc Documentation
articles/ProjectSettings.html.summary: Project Settings UCalc exposes several configuration options that control how expressions are parsed, compiled, and how symbols are discovered. These settings can be found in: Project Settings → UCalc Contexts These settings control how symbol contexts are built and what members are discovered. Context Population Mode Controls how type contexts populate their members. Lazy Members are discovered only when first used during compilation. Benefits: lower memory usage faster initialization This is the default option and generally recommended for most projects. Eager All usable members are discovered immediately when the context is created. Benefits: slightly faster expression compilation fewer runtime lookups Drawback: higher memory usage. Note: This setting only affects runtime builds. The Unity Editor always uses Eager mode to support autocomplete in expression fields. Include Non-Public Static Members When enabled, static member discovery will include: private protected internal members. By default only public static members are exposed. Include Non-Public Instance Members When enabled, instance symbol discovery will include: private protected internal members from the expression owner. This can be useful when expressions need to access internal implementation details. Tokens These settings control how expression tokens are compared. Token Comparison Defines how identifiers are compared during parsing and symbol lookup. Example modes include: Ordinal OrdinalIgnoreCase For example, with OrdinalIgnoreCase: health Health HEALTH would all resolve to the same symbol. This setting also affects: argument names dependency names global symbol providers custom providers Performance These options control internal optimizations used by UCalc. Compile Parlot Parsers When enabled, Parlot parsers are compiled to improve parsing performance. This can significantly reduce the cost of repeatedly parsing expressions. Disabling this option may slightly reduce startup cost but generally results in slower parsing. Compile Using Fast Expressions When enabled, UCalc uses FastExpressionCompiler to compile expression trees. Benefits: faster delegate compilation improved runtime performance If disabled, UCalc will fall back to the standard: Linq.Expression.Compile() method.

articles/ProvidingConstructors.html.title: Providing Constructors | UCalc Documentation
articles/ProvidingConstructors.html.summary: Providing Constructors UCalc allows expressions to create objects using the new keyword. new Vector3(x, y, z) Constructors are provided through global symbol providers. If you want to expose constructors for additional types, you need to create and register your own provider. Creating a Constructor Provider To provide constructors you need to implement: ISymbolProvider ISymbolEnumerator (for editor autocomplete support) The provider is then registered globally using: UCalcContextRegistry.RegisterGlobalProvider(provider); This makes the constructors available to all expressions. Using the Project String Comparer To stay consistent with the rest of UCalc, you should use the string comparer defined in project settings. UCalcProjectSettings.Instance.TokenComparer This ensures constructor tokens behave consistently with the configured case sensitivity rules. Creating Constructor Symbols To expose a constructor to UCalc you must create an ExpressionConstructor. The simplest way is to obtain a ConstructorInfo using reflection: var ctor = typeof(MyType).GetConstructor(new [] { typeof(int), typeof(float) }); var symbol = new ExpressionConstructor(ctor); Once created, the symbol can be returned by your provider when the constructor token is requested. Example Provider Below is a simplified example of a constructor provider that exposes constructors for several Unity types. internal sealed class UnityConstructorsProvider : ISymbolProvider, ISymbolEnumerator { private readonly Dictionary<string, List<ExpressionConstructor>> m_Constructors; private readonly StringComparer m_Comparer; public UnityConstructorsProvider(StringComparer comparer) { m_Comparer = comparer ?? StringComparer.Ordinal; m_Constructors = new Dictionary<string, List<ExpressionConstructor>>(m_Comparer); PopulateDefaults(); } public bool TryGetArgument(string token, out Expression argOrConst) { argOrConst = null; return false; } public bool TryGetProperty(string token, out ExpressionProperty parameter) { parameter = default; return false; } public bool TryGetFunctions(string token, out IReadOnlyList<ExpressionFunction> overloads) { overloads = null; return false; } public bool TryGetConstructors(string token, out IReadOnlyList<ExpressionConstructor> overloads) { if (m_Constructors.TryGetValue(token, out var list) && list.Count > 0) { overloads = list; return true; } overloads = null; return false; } public bool TryGetSubProvider(string token, out ISymbolProvider subProvider) { subProvider = null; return false; } } This provider stores constructors in a dictionary keyed by the type token. Populating Constructors Constructors are typically discovered using reflection: private void AddConstructors(Type type) { var ctors = type.GetConstructors(BindingFlags.Public | BindingFlags.Instance); foreach (var c in ctors) { if (c.IsStatic) continue; if (c.ContainsGenericParameters) continue; list.Add(new ExpressionConstructor(c)); } } Each constructor is wrapped in an ExpressionConstructor, which makes it recognizable by the UCalc compiler. Autocomplete Support To enable constructor suggestions in the editor, implement the enumeration methods from ISymbolEnumerator. Example: public void EnumerateConstructors(List<(string name, IReadOnlyList<ExpressionConstructor> overloads)> dst) { dst.AddRange(m_Constructors.Select(kvp => (kvp.Key, kvp.Value as IReadOnlyList<ExpressionConstructor>))); } This allows the editor to display constructor suggestions while typing expressions. Result Once registered, constructors become available in expressions: new Vector3(x, y, z) new Color(1, 0, 0) The correct constructor overload is selected automatically based on argument types.

index.html.title: UCalc | UCalc Documentation
index.html.summary: UCalc UCalc is a lightweight and efficient expression parser designed specifically for Unity. It allows you to serialize lambda expressions directly in the Unity Inspector, making it easy to expose customizable logic and calculations without writing additional scripts. UCalc integrates seamlessly with the Unity Editor and provides a powerful yet intuitive way to define expressions that are compiled into fast runtime delegates. Why UCalc? UCalc makes it possible to move small pieces of logic into data by allowing expressions to be defined directly in serialized fields. Common use cases include: Configurable formulas Example: damage calculations, score multipliers, stat scaling. Custom gameplay logic Designers can define simple rules without requiring new code. Advanced persistent events Go beyond what UnityEvent offers by allowing serialized lambda listeners. Data-driven systems Expressions can reference context objects, arguments, and dependencies. Unity Editor Integration UCalc is built with Unity workflows in mind. Features include: Serialized lambda expressions Expression validation Autocomplete support Reflection-based symbol discovery Integration with Unity objects and components Expressions are compiled into fast delegates using expression trees, ensuring runtime performance remains excellent. Example A serialized lambda can expose arguments and be written directly in the Inspector: health * damageMultiplier + bonusDamage At runtime this expression is compiled into a delegate equivalent to: (float health, float damageMultiplier, float bonusDamage) => health * damageMultiplier + bonusDamage; Community & Support If you encounter issues or want to discuss the project: 🐞 Report a bug: Bug Tracker 💬 Join discussions: Community Discussions 🛒 View on the Unity Asset Store: Asset Store Page

samples/BasicUsage.html.summary: Basic Usage Sample This sample demonstrates how to use UCalc serialized expressions in a real Unity scene. It shows how to: evaluate a SerializedFunc invoke a UCalcEvent reference dependencies use global symbols use UCalcReadOnly and UCalcHidden write expressions directly in the Inspector Scene Overview The sample scene contains: UI buttons used to invoke expressions a cube that moves based on event logic two Transform reference objects used as target positions The logic is implemented in the UCalc_BasicUsage_Script.cs file. Example Function The sample includes a serialized function: [SerializeField] SerializedFunc<float, float> m_ExampleFunc; Argument name: input Expression used in the sample: Global.Remap10(input) * ExampleReadOnly When you press InvokeFunc, the function is executed with a random value. The result is displayed in the UI. This demonstrates: calling global functions reading owner members passing arguments to expressions Global Symbols The sample includes a custom global provider: [UCalcGlobalContext("Global")] public static class UCalc_BasicUsage_Global { public static float Remap10(float value) => Mathf.Lerp(0, 10, Mathf.Clamp01(value)); } This exposes the function: Global.Remap10(value) which is used in the function example. Example Event The sample also demonstrates UCalcEvent<bool>. [SerializeField] UCalcEvent<bool> m_ExampleEvent; When InvokeEvent is pressed, the event is invoked using the value of the toggle. The persistent listener expression used in the sample: Transform.position = value ? t1.transform.position : t2.transform.position This expression: reads the event argument (value) accesses dependencies (t1, t2) modifies the owner's transform As a result, the cube moves between the two reference positions. Dependencies The event expression references two external objects. These are added as dependencies: Name Object t1 T1 (Game Object) t2 T2 (Game Object) Dependencies allow expressions to access external objects without exposing them on the owner component. Attribute Examples The sample also demonstrates several attributes. UCalcHidden [UCalcHidden] public UIDocument Document; This prevents the member from being available inside expressions despite being public. UCalcReadOnly [UCalcReadOnly] public int ExampleReadOnly; This member can be read by expressions but cannot be modified. ArgNames [ArgNames("input")] Provides a readable argument name for the serialized lambda. Try It Yourself Open the scene and press Play. You can experiment by: modifying the function expression (also during play-mode) adding more event listeners changing dependency objects editing argument names creating new expressions This allows you to quickly explore how UCalc expressions interact with Unity objects.
